frontText: The arrow keys are forbidden. Move the cursor one character **left** — which home-row key?
backText:
```
h
```

The leftmost key of the `h j k l` cluster moves left — easy to remember by position.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: The arrow keys are forbidden. Move the cursor one line **down** — which home-row key?
backText:
```
j
```

Mnemonic: `j` dips below the line like an arrow pointing down.

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText: The arrow keys are forbidden. Move the cursor one line **up** — which home-row key?
backText:
```
k
```

Sits next to `j`; up is its counterpart.

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText: The arrow keys are forbidden. Move the cursor one character **right** — which home-row key?
backText:
```
l
```

The rightmost key of the `h j k l` cluster moves right.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText:
The cursor sits on the `c` of `const`:

```js
const userName = getUser();
```

Land on the `u` of `userName` in **one keystroke**.
backText:
```
w
```

Jumps to the start of the next word. Takes a count: `3w` jumps three words.

[Word motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#word-motions)

frontText:
The cursor sits on the `u` of `userName`:

```js
const userName = getUser();
```

Jump back onto the `c` of `const` in **one keystroke**.
backText:
```
b
```

Jumps to the start of the previous word — the backward counterpart of `w`.

[Word motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#word-motions)

frontText:
The cursor sits on the `u` of `userName`:

```js
const userName = getUser();
```

Land on the **last** character of `userName` (the final `e`) in **one keystroke**.
backText:
```
e
```

Jumps to the end of the word. Useful with operators: `ce` changes to the end of the word.

[Word motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#word-motions)

frontText:
The cursor is in the middle of this indented line:

```js
        return result;
```

Jump to **column 0** — before the indentation — in one keystroke.
backText:
```
0
```

Goes to the very first column, even when the line is indented.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText:
The cursor is at the **end** of this indented line:

```js
        return result;
```

Jump onto the `r` of `return` (the first non-blank character) in one keystroke.
backText:
```
^
```

First non-blank character — usually what you want in indented code, unlike `0`.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: The cursor is at the start of a very long line. Jump to its **last character** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
$
```

End of line. Combines with operators: `d$` (or `D`) deletes to the end of the line.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: You are on line 500 and need to check the imports at the **top of the file**. Get to line 1.
backText:
```
gg
```

First line of the file. With a count it jumps to that line (`1G` does the same).

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText: You are on line 1 of a 2000-line file. Jump to the **very last line** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
G
```

Mnemonic: Go to the end.

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText:
The stack trace says:

```
TypeError at app.js:42
```

`app.js` is already open. Jump **straight to line 42**.
backText:
```
42G
```

`:42` and `42gg` do the same.

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText: You are in the middle of a function body. Hop **up to the blank line above the function** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
{
```

Jumps to the previous empty line (paragraph motion) — great for moving between functions.

[Object motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#object-motions)

frontText: You are in the middle of a function body. Hop **down to the next blank line** (past the end of this block) in one keystroke.
backText:
```
}
```

Jumps to the next empty line — the downward counterpart of `{`.

[Object motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#object-motions)

frontText: Scroll **half a screen down** without losing your reading flow.
backText:
```
Ctrl-d
```

Mnemonic: down. Keeps more visual context than a full-page jump.

[Scrolling](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html)

frontText: Scroll **half a screen up**.
backText:
```
Ctrl-u
```

Mnemonic: up.

[Scrolling](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html)

frontText: Skim quickly through a long file: scroll a **full page forward** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
Ctrl-f
```

Mnemonic: forward.

[Scrolling](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html)

frontText: You skimmed too far: scroll a **full page backward** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
Ctrl-b
```

Mnemonic: backward.

[Scrolling](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html)

frontText: **Without scrolling the view**, move the cursor to the **topmost visible line** of the window.
backText:
```
H
```

Mnemonic: High. Moves the cursor, not the view.

[Various motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#various-motions)

frontText: **Without scrolling the view**, move the cursor to the **middle visible line** of the window.
backText:
```
M
```

Mnemonic: Middle.

[Various motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#various-motions)

frontText: **Without scrolling the view**, move the cursor to the **bottom visible line** of the window.
backText:
```
L
```

Mnemonic: Low.

[Various motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#various-motions)

frontText: The line you're editing is stuck at the very **bottom edge** of the window and you can't see what follows. **Center it on the screen** without moving the cursor off it.
backText:
```
zz
```

`zt` scrolls the current line to the top, `zb` to the bottom.

[Scrolling relative to cursor](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html#scroll-cursor)

frontText:
The cursor is at the start of this line:

```js
const result = compute(value);
```

Jump **directly onto the `=`** with two keystrokes.
backText:
```
f=
```

`f` + any character jumps onto its next occurrence in the line. `F=` searches backward, `t=` stops just before it. Combines with operators: `df=`.

[f motion](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#f)

frontText:
You pressed `f,` and landed on the **first comma** of:

```js
add(first, second, third)
```

Get to the **second comma** with **one keystroke**.
backText:
```
;
```

Repeats the last `f`/`F`/`t`/`T` jump. Press repeatedly to keep hopping.

[; motion](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#%3B)

frontText: You pressed `;` once too many and **overshot the comma** you wanted. Step **back one occurrence** with one keystroke.
backText:
```
,
```

Repeats the last `f`/`F`/`t`/`T` jump in the opposite direction.

[, motion](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#%2C)

frontText: You were editing line 812, pressed `gg` to peek at the imports — now **jump back to exactly where you were**.
backText:
```
Ctrl-o
```

Mnemonic: older position in the jump list. Press repeatedly to go further back.

[Jump motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#jump-motions)

frontText: You pressed `Ctrl-o` one time too many and went too far back in the jump history. Go **forward** again with one keystroke.
backText:
```
Ctrl-i
```

Same key as `Tab` — the counterpart of `Ctrl-o`.

[Jump motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#jump-motions)

frontText: You scrolled far away while reading documentation in the same file. Jump back to **the exact spot of your last edit**.
backText:
```
`.
```

Backtick + dot: the `.` mark always holds the position of the most recent change.

[Marks](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#%60.)

frontText:
Delete this leftover debug line **entirely** (the whole line, not just its text):

```js
console.log('debug');
```
backText:
```
dd
```

The line goes into the unnamed register — `p` afterwards pastes it, so `dd` + `p` moves a line.

[dd](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#dd)

```
3dd
```

Any count works — `10dd` deletes ten lines.

[dd](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#dd)

frontText:
The cursor is on the space right before the comment:

```js
let x = 5; // TODO: remove this
```

Delete **from the cursor to the end of the line** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
D
```

Shorthand for `d$`. `C` does the same but leaves you in Insert mode.

[D](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#D)

frontText: Add a new statement **below** the current line — open the line and be ready to type, in one keystroke.
backText:
```
o
```

Mnemonic: open. Respects the surrounding auto-indentation.

[o](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html#o)

frontText: You're on the first line of the file and need to add a missing import **above** it — open a line above and be ready to type, in one keystroke.
backText:
```
O
```

Capital `o` — opens the line above the cursor.

[O](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html#O)

frontText: **Copy the current line** (you'll paste it elsewhere in a moment).
backText:
```
yy
```

`3yy` yanks three lines. `yyp` duplicates the current line.

[yy](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#yy)

frontText: You copied a line with `yy` and moved to the target spot. **Paste it below** the current line.
backText:
```
p
```

For line-wise yanks (`yy`, `dd`, `V`+`y`) the text is inserted on a new line below.

[p](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#p)

frontText: Same situation, but the copied line must land **above** the current line. Which keystroke?
backText:
```
P
```

Capital `p` — inserts before/above instead of after/below.

[P](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#P)

```
J
```

Joins with a single space at the seam; `gJ` joins without the space; `3J` joins three lines.

[J](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#J)

```
V j j  y   →  move to the closing brace  →  p
```

Line-wise Visual select, yank, then paste below the target line. Use `d` instead of `y` to move instead of copy.

[Visual mode](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html)

frontText: Lines **5–9** contain a config block you need duplicated **after line 20** — do it with a **single Ex command**, no Visual mode, no yanking.
backText:
```vim
:5,9t20
```

`:t` copies a range after the target line. `.` = current line, `$` = last line.

[:copy](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3At)

frontText: Same block, but this time **move** lines 5–9 after line 20 (don't leave the original behind) — one Ex command.
backText:
```vim
:5,9m20
```

Like `:t` but moves instead of copying.

[:move](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Am)

frontText:
The cursor is somewhere on `count`:

```js
count += 1;
```

Search for **every whole-word occurrence** of `count` in the file with a **single keystroke** (no typing the word).
backText:
```
*
```

Searches forward for the word under the cursor, whole words only. `n`/`N` step through the results; `g*` matches partial words too.

[star](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#star)

frontText: Same setup — cursor on `count` — but you want to search **backward** (the earlier occurrences). One keystroke.
backText:
```
#
```

The backward counterpart of `*`.

[#](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%23)

frontText: You pressed `*` on `oldName`. Now **change the next match** in a way that the edit can be replayed on further matches with `.` — which command starts the change?
backText:
```
cgn
```

`gn` selects the next search match, `c` changes it. Type the replacement, press `Esc` — then `.` repeats it on further matches. This is Vim's plugin-free multi-cursor.

[gn](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#gn)

frontText: You just did `* cgn newName Esc`. The next occurrence should get **the same replacement** — one keystroke.
backText:
```
.
```

The dot command replays the last change on the next match. Press `n` first to skip an occurrence you want to keep.

[Repeating](https://vimhelp.org/repeat.txt.html#single-repeat)

frontText: You need to select a **rectangle**: the first two characters of four consecutive lines (not whole lines!). Which keystroke **starts** this kind of selection?
backText:
```
Ctrl-v
```

Visual Block mode selects a rectangle; extend it with `h j k l`, `f<char>`, `$`.

[Visual Block mode](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#visual-block)

```
I
```

Block-insert at the left edge: type `// `, press `Esc`, and it appears on every selected line.

[v_b_I](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_I)

frontText:
A block is selected and extended to the line ends with `$`:

```js
const a = 1
const bb = 22
const ccc = 333
```

Append a `;` to **every line's end**, typing it only once — which key starts the append?
backText:
```
A
```

Block-append after the right edge: type `;`, press `Esc`. After `$` the block is "ragged right", so `A` appends at each line's own end.

[v_b_A](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_A)

frontText: Your `Ctrl-v` block needs to reach **the end of every selected line**, but the lines all have **different lengths**. Which keystroke extends the block that way?
backText:
```
$
```

Inside Visual Block, `$` makes the block follow each line's actual end — then `A` appends there.

[v_b_$](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_%24)

frontText: You block-selected the word `left` in the same column of five lines. **Replace it with `right` on all five lines**, typing the new text only once — which key starts it?
backText:
```
c
```

Block-change: the selection disappears, you type the replacement once, and `Esc` applies it to every line.

[v_b_c](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_c)

frontText: You block-selected an unwanted column (e.g. leading `> ` quote markers on every line). **Delete the column** on all lines — one keystroke.
backText:
```
d
```

`x` does the same in Visual Block mode.

[v_d](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#v_d)

frontText: You block-selected a column of `-` characters. **Overwrite every selected character with `*`** — two keystrokes.
backText:
```
r*
```

In Visual mode, `r` + any character overwrites the whole selection with that character.

[v_r](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#v_r)

frontText:
You block-selected the zeros in:

```
step 0
step 0
step 0
```

Turn them into `1`, `2`, `3` (an increasing sequence) with **one command**.
backText:
```
g Ctrl-a
```

Adds 1 to the first number, 2 to the second, 3 to the third… Plain `Ctrl-a` adds 1 to every number instead.

[v_g_CTRL-A](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#v_g_CTRL-A)

frontText:
This line needs **one more level of indentation**:

```js
return x;
```

Indent it without entering Insert mode.
backText:
```
>>
```

`3>>` indents three lines; the width comes from `shiftwidth`.

[>>](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3E%3E)

frontText: This line is indented **one level too deep**. Un-indent it without entering Insert mode.
backText:
```
<<
```

The counterpart of `>>`.

[<<](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3C%3C)

frontText: You selected five lines with `V`. **Indent them all** one level — one keystroke.
backText:
```
>
```

`<` un-indents. The selection collapses afterwards — `gv` reselects it, or press `.` to indent again.

[v_>](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#v_%3E)

frontText: You indented a selection with `>`, but it needs **one more level** — and the selection is gone. **Bring the exact same selection back** with one command.
backText:
```
gv
```

Restores the previous Visual selection exactly as it was. (Or skip reselecting and just press `.` to repeat the indent.)

[gv](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#gv)

frontText: The current line is indented completely wrong. **Fix its indentation automatically** according to the file type's rules.
backText:
```
==
```

In Visual mode a single `=` auto-indents the selection.

[=](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3D)

frontText: You opened a file whose **entire indentation is mangled**. Re-indent the **whole file** with one command sequence.
backText:
```
gg=G
```

Go to the top (`gg`), auto-indent (`=`) to the end (`G`).

[=](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3D)

frontText: You are **typing in Insert mode**, mid-line, and realize the line needs one more indent level. Indent it **without leaving Insert mode**.
backText:
```
Ctrl-t
```

Works from anywhere in the line.

[i_CTRL-T](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html#i_CTRL-T)

frontText: Still **in Insert mode**: the line is indented one level too deep. Un-indent it **without leaving Insert mode**.
backText:
```
Ctrl-d
```

The Insert-mode counterpart of `Ctrl-t`.

[i_CTRL-D](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html#i_CTRL-D)

frontText: **Config challenge:** pressing `Tab` must insert **spaces, never a tab character**. Which option goes in `~/.vimrc`?
backText:
```vim
set expandtab
```

Pair with `shiftwidth=4`, `tabstop=4`, `softtabstop=4` for consistent 4-space indentation.

['expandtab'](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27expandtab%27)

frontText: A legacy file **mixes tabs and spaces**. Convert its existing tabs to match your current settings — one command.
backText:
```vim
:retab
```

With `expandtab` set, this converts all tab characters to spaces.

[:retab](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Aretab)

frontText: You copied a snippet in the **browser** with `Ctrl-C`. Paste it into Vim at the cursor — which register + keystroke?
backText:
```
"+p
```

The `+` register is the OS clipboard (needs Neovim or Vim with `+clipboard`). Never causes the indent "staircase" that raw terminal paste does.

[quoteplus](https://vimhelp.org/gui_x11.txt.html#quoteplus)

frontText: You visually selected a function and want to **paste it into Slack** afterwards. Copy it to the **system clipboard**.
backText:
```
"+y
```

Select first (e.g. `V` + motions), then `"+y` puts it in the OS clipboard.

[Registers](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#registers)

frontText: On an old Vim over SSH, pasting with the terminal shortcut turns code into a **"staircase"** of ever-deeper indentation. What do you run **before pasting** to prevent it?
backText:
```vim
:set paste
```

Paste, then `:set nopaste` to restore. Modern Vim/Neovim with bracketed paste usually makes this unnecessary.

['paste'](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27paste%27)

frontText: You yanked a line from a deeply nested block and are pasting it at a **shallower** level. Paste it so its **indentation adapts to the current line** — one command.
backText:
```
]p
```

Like `p`, but re-indents the pasted lines to the current context.

[]p](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%5Dp)

frontText: **Config challenge:** make **every** `y`, `d` and `p` use the system clipboard automatically — no `"+` prefix ever again. Which option?
backText:
```vim
set clipboard=unnamedplus
```

Goes in `~/.vimrc`.

['clipboard'](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27clipboard%27)

frontText:
The cursor is on the opening `(` of a long condition:

```js
if (user && (user.age > 18 || user.verified)) {
```

Jump to **its matching `)`** in one keystroke.
backText:
```
%
```

Works on `( ) [ ] { }`; the cursor may be on the bracket or before it on the line. `:packadd! matchit` (ships with Vim) extends it to HTML tags and `if/endif`.

[%](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#%25)

frontText:
The cursor is **anywhere inside** the parentheses:

```js
calculate(a, b, c)
```

Delete **all three arguments** but keep the parens — one command.
backText:
```
di(
```

`i(` = "inner parentheses". Same pattern for `i{` `i[` `i"` `i'` `it`.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText:
Same line, cursor inside the parens:

```js
calculate(a, b, c)
```

Delete the arguments **and start typing the new ones immediately** — one command.
backText:
```
ci(
```

Change-inner: deletes the contents and enters Insert mode. `ci"` / `ci'` do the same inside quotes.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText:
Cursor inside the condition:

```js
if (items.length > 0 && isReady)
```

**Copy the condition** (everything inside the parens, without them) — one command.
backText:
```
yi(
```

Yanks the inner content without the parens themselves.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText:
Cursor inside the parens of:

```js
wrap((a, b) => a + b)
```

Delete the argument list **including the parentheses themselves** — one command. (Assume the cursor is inside the *outer* pair.)
backText:
```
da(
```

`a` = around (includes the pair), `i` = inner (contents only).

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText: The cursor is somewhere inside a long function body. **Visually select the whole body** (everything inside its `{ }`) — one command.
backText:
```
vi{
```

`va{` includes the braces. Pressing `i{` again expands to the enclosing block.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText: You are deep inside nested `if`s and loops. Jump to the `{` that **opens the current block** — one command.
backText:
```
[{
```

Jumps to the previous unmatched `{`; repeat to climb further out. `[(` does the same for parens.

[[{](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#%5B%7B)

frontText: Same nested position — now jump to the `}` that **closes the current block**.
Find the next occurrence of `handleClick` **below** the cursor.
backText:
```
/handleClick
```

Press `Enter`, then `n` for the next match. `set ignorecase smartcase` makes searches case-insensitive unless the pattern has a capital.

[/](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%2F)

frontText: Find the nearest occurrence of `handleClick` **above** the cursor.
backText:
```
?handleClick
```

Like `/` but searches upward.

[?](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%3F)

frontText: You searched with `/pattern` and are on the first match. Jump to the **next match** — one keystroke.
backText:
```
n
```

Repeats the last search in the same direction.

[n](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#n)

frontText: You pressed `n` one time too many. Jump back to the **previous match** — one keystroke.
backText:
```
N
```

Repeats the last search in the opposite direction.

[N](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#N)

frontText: The search is done but every match is **still glowing yellow**. Turn the highlighting off.
backText:
```vim
:noh
```

Short for `:nohlsearch` — highlights return on the next search.

[:nohlsearch](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%3Anohlsearch)

frontText: Rename every `fetchData` to `loadData` **in the whole file** — one command.
backText:
```vim
:%s/fetchData/loadData/g
```

`%` = every line, `g` = all matches per line.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: Same rename, but some occurrences must stay — Vim should **ask you at every match**. One command.
backText:
```vim
:%s/fetchData/loadData/gc
```

At each match: `y` yes, `n` no, `a` all remaining, `q` quit.

[:s_flags](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3As_flags)

frontText: Replace `old` with `new`, but **only between lines 5 and 20**. One command.
backText:
```vim
:5,20s/old/new/g
```

Any range works before `:s` — e.g. `.,+5` for the current and next five lines.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: You selected a block of lines with `V`. Replace `old` with `new` **only inside that selection**.
backText:
```vim
:'<,'>s/old/new/g
```

Pressing `:` while a selection is active inserts the `'<,'>` range automatically.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: Rename the variable `id` to `uuid` in the file — but `identifier` and `valid` must **not** be touched. One command.
backText:
```vim
:%s/\<id\>/uuid/g
```

`\<` and `\>` anchor to word boundaries — the same anchors `*` adds automatically.

[/\<](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%2F%5C%3C)

frontText: Find every `TODO` across **all files of the project** using only built-in Vim. One command.
backText:
```vim
:vimgrep /TODO/ **/*
```

Fills the quickfix list. `:grep -r TODO src/` uses the external grep instead (`set grepprg=rg\ --vimgrep` for ripgrep).

[:vimgrep](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html#%3Avimgrep)

frontText: Your `:vimgrep` finished. **Open the list of all matches** in a window you can browse.
backText:
```vim
:copen
```

Opens the quickfix window; `Enter` on a line jumps to that match.

[:copen](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html#%3Acopen)

frontText: You're fixing quickfix matches one by one. **Jump to the next match** (possibly in another file) — one command.
backText:
```vim
:cnext
```

`:cprev` steps backward. Both move across file boundaries.

[:cnext](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html#%3Acnext)

frontText: **Boss challenge:** your `:grep` filled the quickfix list with every file containing `oldApi`. Replace `oldApi` with `newApi` in **all of those files** and **save each one** — a single command line.
backText:
```vim
:cfdo %s/oldApi/newApi/g | update
```

`:cfdo` runs the command in each file in the quickfix list; `| update` writes each changed file.

[:cfdo](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html#%3Acfdo)

frontText: You pressed `*` on the variable `userId`. Now rename **every whole-word occurrence in the file** to `accountId` — **without typing `userId` again**. One command.
backText:
```vim
:%s//accountId/g
```

An **empty pattern** reuses the last search — and `*` already added whole-word anchors, so `userId` matches won't leak into `userIdList`.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: The cursor is on a variable used deep inside a function. Jump to **where it was declared** in this scope — one command.
backText:
```
gd
```

Mnemonic: goto definition. `gD` searches from the start of the file (global definition).

[gd](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#gd)

frontText: You keep bouncing between `user.js` and `user.test.js` (the two most recent files). **Toggle to the other one** with a single keystroke.
backText:
```
Ctrl-^
```

Also written `Ctrl-6`. Switches between the current and the alternate buffer.

[CTRL-^](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#CTRL-%5E)

frontText: You've opened a dozen files and lost track. **List all open buffers** with their numbers.
backText:
```vim
:ls
```

`%` marks the current buffer, `#` the alternate one.

[:ls](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#%3Als)

frontText: `user_controller.js` is already open in a buffer. Switch to it by typing only the fragment `ctrl` — which command?
backText:
```vim
:b ctrl
```

`:b` matches any substring of a buffer name; `Tab` cycles through multiple matches.

[:buffer](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#%3Abuffer)

frontText: Cycle to the **next buffer** in the buffer list.
backText:
```vim
:bn
```

Short for `:bnext`; wraps around at the end of the list.

[:bnext](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#%3Abnext)

frontText: Cycle to the **previous buffer** in the buffer list.
backText:
```vim
:bp
```

Short for `:bprevious`.

[:bprevious](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#%3Abprevious)

frontText: You're done with the current file. **Close its buffer** without quitting Vim.
backText:
```vim
:bd
```

Short for `:bdelete` — the file stays on disk, only the buffer is unloaded.

[:bdelete](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#%3Abdelete)

frontText: Open the file `src/utils/format.js`, using **Tab completion** so you don't type the whole path.
backText:
```vim
:e src/ut<Tab>
```

`:e` (`:edit`) opens a file; `Tab` completes names (`set wildmenu` gives a visual menu). `:e!` reloads the current file, discarding changes.

[:edit](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#%3Aedit)

frontText: Your `~/.vimrc` already has recursive search and fuzzy completion set up. Somewhere deep in the project lives `src/user_controller.js` — open it by typing just a few characters of its name (**no plugins**).
backText:
```vim
:find usrctl<Tab>
```

`:find` searches every directory in `path`; with fuzzy completion a few characters are enough to match the file.

[:find](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#%3Afind)

frontText: **Config challenge:** `:find format.js` fails because the file is deep in a subdirectory. Which single option makes `:find` search the **whole project tree recursively**?
backText:
```vim
set path+=**
```

`**` matches any number of directory levels, so `:find` (and `gf`) can locate files anywhere below the current directory.

['path'](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27path%27)

frontText: **Config challenge:** make command-line completion match **fuzzily**, so `:find usrctl<Tab>` completes to `user_controller.js` (Vim 9 / Neovim).
backText:
```vim
set wildoptions=fuzzy
```

Pair it with `set wildmenu` for the visual completion menu.

['wildoptions'](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27wildoptions%27)
